title: Internal laying and egg peritonitis in hens
subtitle: Recognising the signs of a hen whose reproductive system has broken down, and when to act.
excerpt: Internal laying and egg peritonitis are among the most common reasons laying hens deteriorate and die in backyard flocks. The signs develop slowly and are easy to overlook until the hen is in serious difficulty. Recognising the early indicators gives more options; by the time abdominal swelling is visible, the prognosis is poor without a vet's help.
sourceNotes: DEFRA Code of Practice for the Welfare of Laying Hens (2018), gov.uk. Merck Veterinary Manual: egg peritonitis in poultry, merckvetmanual.com. Poultryhub.org factsheet on reproductive disorders in laying hens.

glossaryTerms.definition: Inflammation of the abdominal cavity caused by yolk material deposited outside the oviduct. The yolk acts as a culture medium for bacteria, leading to a secondary infection. Characterised by swelling of the abdomen, reduced appetite, and a penguin-like stance as the hen holds herself upright to reduce pressure.

A hen in whom the egg is released from the ovary but transported backward into the abdomen rather than forward down the oviduct. The misrouted yolk accumulates in the abdominal cavity, where it may or may not become infected.

Accumulation of fluid in the abdominal cavity. In laying hens, it often develops secondary to egg peritonitis as the body attempts to dilute and contain the yolk material and bacterial infection. The abdomen feels fluid-filled and fluctuates when pressed gently.

content.text: A hen whose egg-laying mechanism breaks down faces one of several overlapping conditions.
Internal laying
and
egg peritonitis
are closely related and frequently occur together. Both are more common in high-production breeds such as ISA Browns and commercial hybrids, but any laying hen can develop them.
Early signs
Drop in egg production over several weeks with no seasonal explanation.
Hen stands apart from the flock, quieter than usual, feathers slightly fluffed.
Reduced appetite; the hen approaches the feeder but does not eat much.
Pale comb and wattles compared to her normal colouring.
Later signs
Upright, penguin-like posture as the hen holds herself tall to reduce pressure on the distended abdomen.
Swollen, fluid-filled abdomen (
ascites
) that feels heavy and fluctuates gently when the hen is picked up.
Straining in or around the nest box with no egg produced.
Watery or bloody discharge from the vent.
What to do

Separate the affected hen to a quiet pen with easy access to food and water. A hen with mild early signs may partly recover once laying pressure is removed. Once peritonitis is established it rarely resolves without a vet.

A vet can drain the abdominal fluid for short-term relief and check the scale of the infection. Antibiotics may add weeks of comfortable life, but the root cause stays. In most backyard settings the choice is between supportive care for a short time and humane slaughter once quality of life is poor.

attrs.title: Distinguishing egg peritonitis from egg binding

content.text: Egg binding (a fully formed egg stuck in the oviduct) produces similar straining signs. The key distinction: with egg binding you can sometimes feel a firm egg shape when the abdomen is gently palpated, and the hen often resolves within 12 to 24 hours with warmth, lubrication, and rest. Egg peritonitis produces a soft, fluid-filled abdomen with no discrete egg mass. If in doubt, a vet can distinguish the two with a simple physical examination.
Reducing risk in a laying flock

Commercial hybrid hens bred for high egg output get reproductive disorders more often than traditional breeds or dual-purpose birds. This is a breed trade-off, not a management failure. Giving hybrid layers a rest in autumn and winter by cutting artificial light reduces the strain on the system. Traditional breeds that lay fewer eggs per year typically live longer and have fewer problems.

Check the abdomen of every hen in the flock at least once a year as part of a handling check. A slight fullness below the keel bone is normal; a soft, pendulous mass that the hen is clearly aware of is not. Early detection allows a faster decision.
